1. 天冷，故添衣。 It is cold, therefore add a coat.
2. 雨近，是以收衣。 Rain is near, so the clothes are brought in.
3. 路遠，故早行。 The road is long, therefore leave early.
4. 客至，是以掃庭。 Guests are coming, so the courtyard is swept.
5. 水急，故緩步。 The water is swift, therefore walk more carefully.
6. 鍋熱，是以減火。 The pot is too hot, so the flame is lowered.
7. 夜深，故閉門。 The night is deep, therefore the door is shut.
8. 米少，是以省食。 Rice is scarce, so the meal is made modest.
9. 人倦，故少言。 People are tired, therefore speech grows sparse.
10. 事繁，是以先簡。 The tasks are many, so one simplifies first.
11. 風起，故掩窗。 Wind rises, therefore the window is closed.
12. 道滑，是以徐行。 The path is slick, so one walks slowly.
13. 燈暗，故近書。 The lamp is dim, therefore the book is brought closer.
14. 客多，是以添席。 There are many guests, so more seats are added.
15. 聲雜，故低語。 The place is noisy, therefore one speaks softly.
16. 飯熟，是以停火。 The rice is cooked, so the fire is stopped.
17. 時晚，故歸。 It is late, therefore return.
18. 紙薄，是以輕寫。 The paper is thin, so the writing must be light.
19. 人急，故多失。 When a person grows hurried, errors follow.
20. 心定，是以少亂。 When the mind is steady, disorder lessens.

1. 生而不奪，成而不居。 Let it arise without seizing it; let it succeed without claiming it.
2. 作而不擾，成而不誇。 Act without disturbing; complete without boasting.
3. 看而不取，聞而不爭。 See without grabbing; hear without competing.
4. 行而不逼，止而不怠。 Move without pressing; stop without collapsing.
5. 教而不壓，引而不拖。 Teach without pressing down; guide without dragging.
6. 守而不閉，用而不盡。 Guard without sealing shut; use without exhausting.
7. 取而不貪，與而不誇。 Take without greed; give without display.
8. 居而不滿，退而不失。 Dwell without self-satisfaction; withdraw without losing measure.
9. 進而不猛，緩而不廢。 Advance without charging; slow down without neglecting.
10. 知而不露，明而不耀。 Know without showing off; be clear without dazzling.
11. 蓄而不塞，發而不傷。 Store without blockage; release without harm.
12. 問而不窮，答而不滿。 Ask without cornering; answer without overfilling the claim.
13. 得而不恃，失而不亂。 Gain without depending on it; lose without falling into disorder.
14. 近而不狎，遠而不絕。 Come near without becoming careless; stand far without breaking off.
15. 視而不執，聽而不隨。 See without clinging; hear without blindly following.
16. 為而不重，止而不空。 Act without overburdening; stop without emptiness.
17. 收而不吝，放而不散。 Gather without stinginess; release without scattering.
18. 養而不縱，裁而不傷。 Nourish without indulgence; trim without damaging the root.
19. 成而不據，久而不倦。 Accomplish without occupying; continue without weariness of self-display.
20. 興而不驕，衰而不亂。 Rise without arrogance; decline without chaos.

1. 茶可茶，豈好茶。 Tea that barely counts as tea — how could it already be good tea?
2. 酒可酒，安得為佳酒。 Wine that merely counts as wine — how could it be fine wine?
3. 飯可飯，何為佳飯。 A meal that merely counts as food — why call it a fine meal?
4. 屋可屋，豈安屋。 A house that barely counts as a house — how could it already be comfortable?
5. 車可車，安得為快車。 A vehicle that merely runs — how could it be a fast car?
6. 書可書，何為佳書。 A text that merely counts as a book — why call it a fine book?
7. 筆可筆，豈妙筆。 A pen that is merely a pen — how could it already be a marvelous pen?
8. 店可店，安得為好店。 A place that merely counts as a shop — how could it already be a good shop?
9. 鞋可鞋，何為佳鞋。 Shoes that merely count as shoes — why call them fine shoes?
10. 網可網，豈快網。 Internet that merely connects — how could it be fast internet?
11. 課可課，安得為好課。 A class that merely exists — how could it already be a good class?
12. 床可床，何為好床。 A bed that merely serves as a bed — why call it a good bed?
13. 屋可屋，安得為久屋。 A house that merely stands — how could it already be long-lasting?
14. 友可友，豈知己。 A friend is a friend — how could every friend be a soulmate?
15. 學可學，安得為深學。 To have studied at all — how could that alone count as deep learning?
16. 行可行，何為遠行。 To be able to walk — why call it a long journey already?
17. 聲可聲，豈妙音。 A sound is a sound — how could it already be beautiful music?
18. 字可字，安得為好字。 Writing that merely forms characters — how could it already be good writing?
19. 門可門，何為名門。 A gate is a gate — why call it a distinguished house?
20. 名可名，安得為真名。 A name that can be spoken — how could it already be the true name?

1. 飯不香，未必不可食。 Rice that is not fragrant is not therefore inedible.
2. 門不新，未為不好門。 A door that is not new is not therefore a bad door.
3. 衣不華，未必不暖。 Clothes that are not fancy are not necessarily not warm.
4. 店不大，未必不佳。 A shop that is not large is not necessarily not good.
5. 字不多，未為無意。 Few words do not mean no meaning.
6. 路不平，未必不通。 A road that is uneven is not therefore impassable.
7. 杯不滿，未必不足。 A cup not filled to the brim is not necessarily insufficient.
8. 屋不高，未為不安。 A house that is not tall is not therefore uncomfortable.
9. 人不語，未必無心。 A person who does not speak is not necessarily without feeling.
10. 茶不濃，未必不醒。 Tea that is not strong is not necessarily not refreshing.
11. 書不厚，未必不深。 A book that is not thick is not necessarily shallow.
12. 車不快，未必不達。 A car that is not fast still may arrive.
13. 人不先，未為不成。 Not being first does not mean failure.
14. 聲不高，未必不聞。 A low voice is not necessarily unheard.
15. 屋不寬，未必不居。 A narrow house may still be livable.
16. 筆不貴，未必不善。 A pen that is not expensive is not necessarily poor.
17. 人不忙，未為無功。 Someone who does not look busy is not therefore unproductive.
18. 光不強，未必不明。 Light that is not harsh may still be clear enough.
19. 言不多，未為無見。 Few words do not mean no insight.
20. 步不疾，未必不遠。 To walk without haste does not mean one cannot go far.

13. 燈暗，故近書。 The lamp is dim, therefore the book is brought closer.
14. 客多，是以添席。 There are many guests, so more seats are added.
15. 聲雜，故低語。 The place is noisy, therefore one speaks softly.
16. 飯熟，是以停火。 The rice is cooked, so the fire is stopped.
17. 時晚，故歸。 It is late, therefore return.
18. 紙薄，是以輕寫。 The paper is thin, so the writing must be light.
19. 人急，故多失。 When a person grows hurried, errors follow.
20. 心定，是以少亂。 When the mind is steady, disorder lessens.

1. 店有店樣，人有人情。 A shop has the form of a shop; a person has human feeling.
2. 飯有飯香，茶有茶味。 Rice has the smell of rice; tea has the taste of tea.
3. 書有書氣，字有字骨。 A book has bookish air; writing has its own backbone.
4. 門有門向，窗有窗光。 A door has its opening; a window has its light.
5. 衣有衣裁，屋有屋勢。 Clothing has its cut; a house has its structure.
6. 車有車路，人有人步。 Cars have car-roads; people have human steps.
7. 山有山色，水有水聲。 Mountains have mountain-color; water has water-sound.
8. 筆有筆意，墨有墨性。 The brush has its gesture; ink has its own nature.
9. 課有課法，學有學程。 Classes have their methods; learning has its pace.
10. 市有市聲，夜有夜靜。 The market has its noise; the night has its stillness.
11. 屋有屋影，燈有燈圈。 A house has its shadows; a lamp has its circle of light.
12. 雨有雨腳，風有風路。 Rain has its falling lines; wind too has its path.
13. 人有人氣，器有器用。 People have human presence; tools have use.
14. 茶有茶候，酒有酒性。 Tea has its proper timing; wine has its own tendency.
15. 木有木理，石有石紋。 Wood has grain; stone has veining.
16. 遠有遠意，近有近情。 Distance has one meaning; nearness has another feeling.
17. 手有手感，眼有眼力。 Hands have touch; eyes have discernment.
18. 早有早光，晚有晚色。 Morning has morning light; evening has evening color.
19. 舊有舊味，新有新意。 Old things have old flavor; new things have new intention.
20. 動有動勢，靜有靜心。 Motion has its momentum; stillness has its own mind.

1. 飯不香，未必不可食。 Rice that is not fragrant is not therefore inedible.
2. 門不新，未為不好門。 A door that is not new is not therefore a bad door.
3. 衣不華，未必不暖。 Clothes that are not fancy are not necessarily not warm.
4. 店不大，未必不佳。 A shop that is not large is not necessarily not good.
5. 字不多，未為無意。 Few words do not mean no meaning.
6. 路不平，未必不通。 A road that is uneven is not therefore impassable.
7. 杯不滿，未必不足。 A cup not filled to the brim is not necessarily insufficient.
8. 屋不高，未為不安。 A house that is not tall is not therefore uncomfortable.
9. 人不語，未必無心。 A person who does not speak is not necessarily without feeling.
10. 茶不濃，未必不醒。 Tea that is not strong is not necessarily not refreshing.
11. 書不厚，未必不深。 A book that is not thick is not necessarily shallow.
12. 車不快，未必不達。 A car that is not fast still may arrive.
13. 人不先，未為不成。 Not being first does not mean failure.
14. 聲不高，未必不聞。 A low voice is not necessarily unheard.
15. 屋不寬，未必不居。 A narrow house may still be livable.
16. 筆不貴，未必不善。 A pen that is not expensive is not necessarily poor.
17. 人不忙，未為無功。 Someone who does not look busy is not therefore unproductive.
18. 光不強，未必不明。 Light that is not harsh may still be clear enough.
19. 言不多，未為無見。 Few words do not mean no insight.
20. 步不疾，未必不遠。 To walk without haste does not mean one cannot go far.

1. 良師少言，而人自明。 A good teacher speaks little, and people understand more by themselves.
2. 善醫不驚，而病自緩。 A good physician does not alarm first, and the illness settles.
3. 巧匠不躁，而器自成。 A skilled craftsperson is not restless, and the work comes together.
4. 明主不擾，而事自理。 A clear leader does not meddle, and affairs arrange themselves.
5. 善廚不爭，而味自和。 A good cook does not compete with the ingredients, and the flavors harmonize.
6. 佳友不逼，而情自近。 A true friend does not press, and closeness grows on its own.
7. 長者不誇，而眾自服。 An elder who does not boast is trusted more readily.
8. 善讀不急，而義自出。 A good reader does not rush, and the meaning emerges.
9. 良農不貪，而田自厚。 A good farmer does not grasp too hard, and the field grows rich.
10. 善商不欺，而客自來。 A good merchant does not cheat, and customers return on their own.
11. 善工不亂，而序自見。 A capable worker does not move chaotically, and order shows itself.
12. 善聽不奪，而言自盡。 A good listener does not seize the floor, and speech completes itself.
13. 善寫不飾，而意自真。 A good writer does not over-ornament, and the meaning stays true.
14. 善教不逼，而學自進。 Good teaching does not force, and learning advances by itself.
15. 善管不繁，而眾自安。 Good governance is not over-complicated, and people settle.
16. 善行不喧，而德自見。 Good action does not need noise; character shows itself.
17. 善守不閉，而室自安。 One who guards well does not merely shut things tight, and the house stays safe.
18. 善帶不拉，而眾自齊。 A good guide does not drag people along, and the group keeps together.
19. 善學不滿，而智自長。 A good learner does not feel full too soon, and understanding grows.
20. 善用不耗，而物自久。 To use things well is not to exhaust them, and so they last.

1. 店有店樣，人有人情。 A shop has the form of a shop; a person has human feeling.
2. 飯有飯香，茶有茶味。 Rice has the smell of rice; tea has the taste of tea.
3. 書有書氣，字有字骨。 A book has bookish air; writing has its own backbone.
4. 門有門向，窗有窗光。 A door has its opening; a window has its light.
5. 衣有衣裁，屋有屋勢。 Clothing has its cut; a house has its structure.
6. 車有車路，人有人步。 Cars have car-roads; people have human steps.
7. 山有山色，水有水聲。 Mountains have mountain-color; water has water-sound.
8. 筆有筆意，墨有墨性。 The brush has its gesture; ink has its own nature.
9. 課有課法，學有學程。 Classes have their methods; learning has its pace.
10. 市有市聲，夜有夜靜。 The market has its noise; the night has its stillness.
11. 屋有屋影，燈有燈圈。 A house has its shadows; a lamp has its circle of light.
12. 雨有雨腳，風有風路。 Rain has its falling lines; wind too has its path.
13. 人有人氣，器有器用。 People have human presence; tools have use.
14. 茶有茶候，酒有酒性。 Tea has its proper timing; wine has its own tendency.
15. 木有木理，石有石紋。 Wood has grain; stone has veining.
16. 遠有遠意，近有近情。 Distance has one meaning; nearness has another feeling.
17. 手有手感，眼有眼力。 Hands have touch; eyes have discernment.
18. 早有早光，晚有晚色。 Morning has morning light; evening has evening color.
19. 舊有舊味，新有新意。 Old things have old flavor; new things have new intention.
20. 動有動勢，靜有靜心。 Motion has its momentum; stillness has its own mind.

1. 天冷，故添衣。 It is cold, therefore add a coat.
2. 雨近，是以收衣。 Rain is near, so the clothes are brought in.
3. 路遠，故早行。 The road is long, therefore leave early.
4. 客至，是以掃庭。 Guests are coming, so the courtyard is swept.
5. 水急，故緩步。 The water is swift, therefore walk more carefully.
6. 鍋熱，是以減火。 The pot is too hot, so the flame is lowered.
7. 夜深，故閉門。 The night is deep, therefore the door is shut.
8. 米少，是以省食。 Rice is scarce, so the meal is made modest.
9. 人倦，故少言。 People are tired, therefore speech grows sparse.
10. 事繁，是以先簡。 The tasks are many, so one simplifies first.
11. 風起，故掩窗。 Wind rises, therefore the window is closed.
12. 道滑，是以徐行。 The path is slick, so one walks slowly.
13. 燈暗，故近書。 The lamp is dim, therefore the book is brought closer.
14. 客多，是以添席。 There are many guests, so more seats are added.
15. 聲雜，故低語。 The place is noisy, therefore one speaks softly.
16. 飯熟，是以停火。 The rice is cooked, so the fire is stopped.
17. 時晚，故歸。 It is late, therefore return.
18. 紙薄，是以輕寫。 The paper is thin, so the writing must be light.
19. 人急，故多失。 When a person grows hurried, errors follow.
20. 心定，是以少亂。 When the mind is steady, disorder lessens.

1. 飯不香，未必不可食。 Rice that is not fragrant is not therefore inedible.
2. 門不新，未為不好門。 A door that is not new is not therefore a bad door.
3. 衣不華，未必不暖。 Clothes that are not fancy are not necessarily not warm.
4. 店不大，未必不佳。 A shop that is not large is not necessarily not good.
5. 字不多，未為無意。 Few words do not mean no meaning.
6. 路不平，未必不通。 A road that is uneven is not therefore impassable.
7. 杯不滿，未必不足。 A cup not filled to the brim is not necessarily insufficient.
8. 屋不高，未為不安。 A house that is not tall is not therefore uncomfortable.
9. 人不語，未必無心。 A person who does not speak is not necessarily without feeling.
10. 茶不濃，未必不醒。 Tea that is not strong is not necessarily not refreshing.
11. 書不厚，未必不深。 A book that is not thick is not necessarily shallow.
12. 車不快，未必不達。 A car that is not fast still may arrive.
13. 人不先，未為不成。 Not being first does not mean failure.
14. 聲不高，未必不聞。 A low voice is not necessarily unheard.
15. 屋不寬，未必不居。 A narrow house may still be livable.
16. 筆不貴，未必不善。 A pen that is not expensive is not necessarily poor.
17. 人不忙，未為無功。 Someone who does not look busy is not therefore unproductive.
18. 光不強，未必不明。 Light that is not harsh may still be clear enough.
19. 言不多，未為無見。 Few words do not mean no insight.
20. 步不疾，未必不遠。 To walk without haste does not mean one cannot go far.

1. 茶可茶，豈好茶。 Tea that barely counts as tea — how could it already be good tea?
2. 酒可酒，安得為佳酒。 Wine that merely counts as wine — how could it be fine wine?
3. 飯可飯，何為佳飯。 A meal that merely counts as food — why call it a fine meal?
4. 屋可屋，豈安屋。 A house that barely counts as a house — how could it already be comfortable?
5. 車可車，安得為快車。 A vehicle that merely runs — how could it be a fast car?
6. 書可書，何為佳書。 A text that merely counts as a book — why call it a fine book?
7. 筆可筆，豈妙筆。 A pen that is merely a pen — how could it already be a marvelous pen?
8. 店可店，安得為好店。 A place that merely counts as a shop — how could it already be a good shop?
9. 鞋可鞋，何為佳鞋。 Shoes that merely count as shoes — why call them fine shoes?
10. 網可網，豈快網。 Internet that merely connects — how could it be fast internet?
11. 課可課，安得為好課。 A class that merely exists — how could it already be a good class?
12. 床可床，何為好床。 A bed that merely serves as a bed — why call it a good bed?
13. 屋可屋，安得為久屋。 A house that merely stands — how could it already be long-lasting?
14. 友可友，豈知己。 A friend is a friend — how could every friend be a soulmate?
15. 學可學，安得為深學。 To have studied at all — how could that alone count as deep learning?
16. 行可行，何為遠行。 To be able to walk — why call it a long journey already?
17. 聲可聲，豈妙音。 A sound is a sound — how could it already be beautiful music?
18. 字可字，安得為好字。 Writing that merely forms characters — how could it already be good writing?
19. 門可門，何為名門。 A gate is a gate — why call it a distinguished house?
20. 名可名，安得為真名。 A name that can be spoken — how could it already be the true name?

14. 聲不高，未必不聞。 A low voice is not necessarily unheard.
15. 屋不寬，未必不居。 A narrow house may still be livable.
16. 筆不貴，未必不善。 A pen that is not expensive is not necessarily poor.
17. 人不忙，未為無功。 Someone who does not look busy is not therefore unproductive.
18. 光不強，未必不明。 Light that is not harsh may still be clear enough.
19. 言不多，未為無見。 Few words do not mean no insight.
20. 步不疾，未必不遠。 To walk without haste does not mean one cannot go far.

1. 天冷，故添衣。 It is cold, therefore add a coat.
2. 雨近，是以收衣。 Rain is near, so the clothes are brought in.
3. 路遠，故早行。 The road is long, therefore leave early.
4. 客至，是以掃庭。 Guests are coming, so the courtyard is swept.
5. 水急，故緩步。 The water is swift, therefore walk more carefully.
6. 鍋熱，是以減火。 The pot is too hot, so the flame is lowered.
7. 夜深，故閉門。 The night is deep, therefore the door is shut.
8. 米少，是以省食。 Rice is scarce, so the meal is made modest.
9. 人倦，故少言。 People are tired, therefore speech grows sparse.
10. 事繁，是以先簡。 The tasks are many, so one simplifies first.
11. 風起，故掩窗。 Wind rises, therefore the window is closed.
12. 道滑，是以徐行。 The path is slick, so one walks slowly.
13. 燈暗，故近書。 The lamp is dim, therefore the book is brought closer.
14. 客多，是以添席。 There are many guests, so more seats are added.
15. 聲雜，故低語。 The place is noisy, therefore one speaks softly.
16. 飯熟，是以停火。 The rice is cooked, so the fire is stopped.
17. 時晚，故歸。 It is late, therefore return.
18. 紙薄，是以輕寫。 The paper is thin, so the writing must be light.
19. 人急，故多失。 When a person grows hurried, errors follow.
20. 心定，是以少亂。 When the mind is steady, disorder lessens.

1. 店有店樣，人有人情。 A shop has the form of a shop; a person has human feeling.
2. 飯有飯香，茶有茶味。 Rice has the smell of rice; tea has the taste of tea.
3. 書有書氣，字有字骨。 A book has bookish air; writing has its own backbone.
4. 門有門向，窗有窗光。 A door has its opening; a window has its light.
5. 衣有衣裁，屋有屋勢。 Clothing has its cut; a house has its structure.
6. 車有車路，人有人步。 Cars have car-roads; people have human steps.
7. 山有山色，水有水聲。 Mountains have mountain-color; water has water-sound.
8. 筆有筆意，墨有墨性。 The brush has its gesture; ink has its own nature.
9. 課有課法，學有學程。 Classes have their methods; learning has its pace.
10. 市有市聲，夜有夜靜。 The market has its noise; the night has its stillness.
11. 屋有屋影，燈有燈圈。 A house has its shadows; a lamp has its circle of light.
12. 雨有雨腳，風有風路。 Rain has its falling lines; wind too has its path.
13. 人有人氣，器有器用。 People have human presence; tools have use.
14. 茶有茶候，酒有酒性。 Tea has its proper timing; wine has its own tendency.
15. 木有木理，石有石紋。 Wood has grain; stone has veining.
16. 遠有遠意，近有近情。 Distance has one meaning; nearness has another feeling.
17. 手有手感，眼有眼力。 Hands have touch; eyes have discernment.
18. 早有早光，晚有晚色。 Morning has morning light; evening has evening color.
19. 舊有舊味，新有新意。 Old things have old flavor; new things have new intention.
20. 動有動勢，靜有靜心。 Motion has its momentum; stillness has its own mind.

1. 天冷，故添衣。 It is cold, therefore add a coat.
2. 雨近，是以收衣。 Rain is near, so the clothes are brought in.
3. 路遠，故早行。 The road is long, therefore leave early.
4. 客至，是以掃庭。 Guests are coming, so the courtyard is swept.
5. 水急，故緩步。 The water is swift, therefore walk more carefully.
6. 鍋熱，是以減火。 The pot is too hot, so the flame is lowered.
7. 夜深，故閉門。 The night is deep, therefore the door is shut.
8. 米少，是以省食。 Rice is scarce, so the meal is made modest.
9. 人倦，故少言。 People are tired, therefore speech grows sparse.
10. 事繁，是以先簡。 The tasks are many, so one simplifies first.
11. 風起，故掩窗。 Wind rises, therefore the window is closed.
12. 道滑，是以徐行。 The path is slick, so one walks slowly.
13. 燈暗，故近書。 The lamp is dim, therefore the book is brought closer.
14. 客多，是以添席。 There are many guests, so more seats are added.
15. 聲雜，故低語。 The place is noisy, therefore one speaks softly.
16. 飯熟，是以停火。 The rice is cooked, so the fire is stopped.
17. 時晚，故歸。 It is late, therefore return.
18. 紙薄，是以輕寫。 The paper is thin, so the writing must be light.
19. 人急，故多失。 When a person grows hurried, errors follow.
20. 心定，是以少亂。 When the mind is steady, disorder lessens.

1. 飯不香，未必不可食。 Rice that is not fragrant is not therefore inedible.
2. 門不新，未為不好門。 A door that is not new is not therefore a bad door.
3. 衣不華，未必不暖。 Clothes that are not fancy are not necessarily not warm.
4. 店不大，未必不佳。 A shop that is not large is not necessarily not good.
5. 字不多，未為無意。 Few words do not mean no meaning.
6. 路不平，未必不通。 A road that is uneven is not therefore impassable.
7. 杯不滿，未必不足。 A cup not filled to the brim is not necessarily insufficient.
8. 屋不高，未為不安。 A house that is not tall is not therefore uncomfortable.
9. 人不語，未必無心。 A person who does not speak is not necessarily without feeling.
10. 茶不濃，未必不醒。 Tea that is not strong is not necessarily not refreshing.
11. 書不厚，未必不深。 A book that is not thick is not necessarily shallow.
12. 車不快，未必不達。 A car that is not fast still may arrive.
13. 人不先，未為不成。 Not being first does not mean failure.
14. 聲不高，未必不聞。 A low voice is not necessarily unheard.
15. 屋不寬，未必不居。 A narrow house may still be livable.
16. 筆不貴，未必不善。 A pen that is not expensive is not necessarily poor.
17. 人不忙，未為無功。 Someone who does not look busy is not therefore unproductive.
18. 光不強，未必不明。 Light that is not harsh may still be clear enough.
19. 言不多，未為無見。 Few words do not mean no insight.
20. 步不疾，未必不遠。 To walk without haste does not mean one cannot go far.

1. 生而不奪，成而不居。 Let it arise without seizing it; let it succeed without claiming it.
2. 作而不擾，成而不誇。 Act without disturbing; complete without boasting.
3. 看而不取，聞而不爭。 See without grabbing; hear without competing.
4. 行而不逼，止而不怠。 Move without pressing; stop without collapsing.
5. 教而不壓，引而不拖。 Teach without pressing down; guide without dragging.
6. 守而不閉，用而不盡。 Guard without sealing shut; use without exhausting.
7. 取而不貪，與而不誇。 Take without greed; give without display.
8. 居而不滿，退而不失。 Dwell without self-satisfaction; withdraw without losing measure.
9. 進而不猛，緩而不廢。 Advance without charging; slow down without neglecting.
10. 知而不露，明而不耀。 Know without showing off; be clear without dazzling.
11. 蓄而不塞，發而不傷。 Store without blockage; release without harm.
12. 問而不窮，答而不滿。 Ask without cornering; answer without overfilling the claim.
13. 得而不恃，失而不亂。 Gain without depending on it; lose without falling into disorder.
14. 近而不狎，遠而不絕。 Come near without becoming careless; stand far without breaking off.
15. 視而不執，聽而不隨。 See without clinging; hear without blindly following.
16. 為而不重，止而不空。 Act without overburdening; stop without emptiness.
17. 收而不吝，放而不散。 Gather without stinginess; release without scattering.
18. 養而不縱，裁而不傷。 Nourish without indulgence; trim without damaging the root.
19. 成而不據，久而不倦。 Accomplish without occupying; continue without weariness of self-display.
20. 興而不驕，衰而不亂。 Rise without arrogance; decline without chaos.

1. 良師少言，而人自明。 A good teacher speaks little, and people understand more by themselves.
2. 善醫不驚，而病自緩。 A good physician does not alarm first, and the illness settles.
3. 巧匠不躁，而器自成。 A skilled craftsperson is not restless, and the work comes together.
4. 明主不擾，而事自理。 A clear leader does not meddle, and affairs arrange themselves.
5. 善廚不爭，而味自和。 A good cook does not compete with the ingredients, and the flavors harmonize.
6. 佳友不逼，而情自近。 A true friend does not press, and closeness grows on its own.
7. 長者不誇，而眾自服。 An elder who does not boast is trusted more readily.
8. 善讀不急，而義自出。 A good reader does not rush, and the meaning emerges.
9. 良農不貪，而田自厚。 A good farmer does not grasp too hard, and the field grows rich.
10. 善商不欺，而客自來。 A good merchant does not cheat, and customers return on their own.
11. 善工不亂，而序自見。 A capable worker does not move chaotically, and order shows itself.
12. 善聽不奪，而言自盡。 A good listener does not seize the floor, and speech completes itself.
13. 善寫不飾，而意自真。 A good writer does not over-ornament, and the meaning stays true.
14. 善教不逼，而學自進。 Good teaching does not force, and learning advances by itself.
15. 善管不繁，而眾自安。 Good governance is not over-complicated, and people settle.
16. 善行不喧，而德自見。 Good action does not need noise; character shows itself.
17. 善守不閉，而室自安。 One who guards well does not merely shut things tight, and the house stays safe.
18. 善帶不拉，而眾自齊。 A good guide does not drag people along, and the group keeps together.
19. 善學不滿，而智自長。 A good learner does not feel full too soon, and understanding grows.
20. 善用不耗，而物自久。 To use things well is not to exhaust them, and so they last.

17. 手有手感，眼有眼力。 Hands have touch; eyes have discernment.
18. 早有早光，晚有晚色。 Morning has morning light; evening has evening color.
19. 舊有舊味，新有新意。 Old things have old flavor; new things have new intention.
20. 動有動勢，靜有靜心。 Motion has its momentum; stillness has its own mind.

1. 店有店樣，人有人情。 A shop has the form of a shop; a person has human feeling.

6. 車有車路，人有人步。 Cars have car-roads; people have human steps.
7. 山有山色，水有水聲。 Mountains have mountain-color; water has water-sound.
8. 筆有筆意，墨有墨性。 The brush has its gesture; ink has its own nature.
9. 課有課法，學有學程。 Classes have their methods; learning has its pace.
10. 市有市聲，夜有夜靜。 The market has its noise; the night has its stillness.
11. 屋有屋影，燈有燈圈。 A house has its shadows; a lamp has its circle of light.
12. 雨有雨腳，風有風路。 Rain has its falling lines; wind too has its path.
13. 人有人氣，器有器用。 People have human presence; tools have use.
14. 茶有茶候，酒有酒性。 Tea has its proper timing; wine has its own tendency.

1. 天冷，故添衣。 It is cold, therefore add a coat.
2. 雨近，是以收衣。 Rain is near, so the clothes are brought in.
3. 路遠，故早行。 The road is long, therefore leave early.
4. 客至，是以掃庭。 Guests are coming, so the courtyard is swept.
5. 水急，故緩步。 The water is swift, therefore walk more carefully.
6. 鍋熱，是以減火。 The pot is too hot, so the flame is lowered.
7. 夜深，故閉門。 The night is deep, therefore the door is shut.
8. 米少，是以省食。 Rice is scarce, so the meal is made modest.
9. 人倦，故少言。 People are tired, therefore speech grows sparse.
10. 事繁，是以先簡。 The tasks are many, so one simplifies first.
11. 風起，故掩窗。 Wind rises, therefore the window is closed.
12. 道滑，是以徐行。 The path is slick, so one walks slowly.
13. 燈暗，故近書。 The lamp is dim, therefore the book is brought closer.
14. 客多，是以添席。 There are many guests, so more seats are added.
15. 聲雜，故低語。 The place is noisy, therefore one speaks softly.
16. 飯熟，是以停火。 The rice is cooked, so the fire is stopped.
17. 時晚，故歸。 It is late, therefore return.
18. 紙薄，是以輕寫。 The paper is thin, so the writing must be light.
19. 人急，故多失。 When a person grows hurried, errors follow.
20. 心定，是以少亂。 When the mind is steady, disorder lessens.

1. 飯不香，未必不可食。 Rice that is not fragrant is not therefore inedible.
2. 門不新，未為不好門。 A door that is not new is not therefore a bad door.
3. 衣不華，未必不暖。 Clothes that are not fancy are not necessarily not warm.
4. 店不大，未必不佳。 A shop that is not large is not necessarily not good.
5. 字不多，未為無意。 Few words do not mean no meaning.
6. 路不平，未必不通。 A road that is uneven is not therefore impassable.
7. 杯不滿，未必不足。 A cup not filled to the brim is not necessarily insufficient.
8. 屋不高，未為不安。 A house that is not tall is not therefore uncomfortable.
9. 人不語，未必無心。 A person who does not speak is not necessarily without feeling.
10. 茶不濃，未必不醒。 Tea that is not strong is not necessarily not refreshing.
11. 書不厚，未必不深。 A book that is not thick is not necessarily shallow.
12. 車不快，未必不達。 A car that is not fast still may arrive.
13. 人不先，未為不成。 Not being first does not mean failure.
14. 聲不高，未必不聞。 A low voice is not necessarily unheard.
15. 屋不寬，未必不居。 A narrow house may still be livable.
16. 筆不貴，未必不善。 A pen that is not expensive is not necessarily poor.
17. 人不忙，未為無功。 Someone who does not look busy is not therefore unproductive.
18. 光不強，未必不明。 Light that is not harsh may still be clear enough.
19. 言不多，未為無見。 Few words do not mean no insight.
20. 步不疾，未必不遠。 To walk without haste does not mean one cannot go far.

1. 生而不奪，成而不居。 Let it arise without seizing it; let it succeed without claiming it.
2. 作而不擾，成而不誇。 Act without disturbing; complete without boasting.
3. 看而不取，聞而不爭。 See without grabbing; hear without competing.
4. 行而不逼，止而不怠。 Move without pressing; stop without collapsing.
5. 教而不壓，引而不拖。 Teach without pressing down; guide without dragging.
6. 守而不閉，用而不盡。 Guard without sealing shut; use without exhausting.
7. 取而不貪，與而不誇。 Take without greed; give without display.
8. 居而不滿，退而不失。 Dwell without self-satisfaction; withdraw without losing measure.
9. 進而不猛，緩而不廢。 Advance without charging; slow down without neglecting.
10. 知而不露，明而不耀。 Know without showing off; be clear without dazzling.
11. 蓄而不塞，發而不傷。 Store without blockage; release without harm.
12. 問而不窮，答而不滿。 Ask without cornering; answer without overfilling the claim.
13. 得而不恃，失而不亂。 Gain without depending on it; lose without falling into disorder.
14. 近而不狎，遠而不絕。 Come near without becoming careless; stand far without breaking off.
15. 視而不執，聽而不隨。 See without clinging; hear without blindly following.
16. 為而不重，止而不空。 Act without overburdening; stop without emptiness.
17. 收而不吝，放而不散。 Gather without stinginess; release without scattering.
18. 養而不縱，裁而不傷。 Nourish without indulgence; trim without damaging the root.
19. 成而不據，久而不倦。 Accomplish without occupying; continue without weariness of self-display.
20. 興而不驕，衰而不亂。 Rise without arrogance; decline without chaos.

1. 飯不香，未必不可食。 Rice that is not fragrant is not therefore inedible.
2. 門不新，未為不好門。 A door that is not new is not therefore a bad door.
3. 衣不華，未必不暖。 Clothes that are not fancy are not necessarily not warm.

8. 屋不高，未為不安。 A house that is not tall is not therefore uncomfortable.

19. 言不多，未為無見。 Few words do not mean no insight.
20. 步不疾，未必不遠。 To walk without haste does not mean one cannot go far.

1. 生而不奪，成而不居。 Let it arise without seizing it; let it succeed without claiming it.
2. 作而不擾，成而不誇。 Act without disturbing; complete without boasting.
3. 看而不取，聞而不爭。 See without grabbing; hear without competing.
4. 行而不逼，止而不怠。 Move without pressing; stop without collapsing.
5. 教而不壓，引而不拖。 Teach without pressing down; guide without dragging.
6. 守而不閉，用而不盡。 Guard without sealing shut; use without exhausting.
7. 取而不貪，與而不誇。 Take without greed; give without display.
8. 居而不滿，退而不失。 Dwell without self-satisfaction; withdraw without losing measure.
9. 進而不猛，緩而不廢。 Advance without charging; slow down without neglecting.
10. 知而不露，明而不耀。 Know without showing off; be clear without dazzling.
11. 蓄而不塞，發而不傷。 Store without blockage; release without harm.
12. 問而不窮，答而不滿。 Ask without cornering; answer without overfilling the claim.
13. 得而不恃，失而不亂。 Gain without depending on it; lose without falling into disorder.
14. 近而不狎，遠而不絕。 Come near without becoming careless; stand far without breaking off.
15. 視而不執，聽而不隨。 See without clinging; hear without blindly following.
16. 為而不重，止而不空。 Act without overburdening; stop without emptiness.
17. 收而不吝，放而不散。 Gather without stinginess; release without scattering.
18. 養而不縱，裁而不傷。 Nourish without indulgence; trim without damaging the root.
19. 成而不據，久而不倦。 Accomplish without occupying; continue without weariness of self-display.
20. 興而不驕，衰而不亂。 Rise without arrogance; decline without chaos.

1. 生而不奪，成而不居。 Let it arise without seizing it; let it succeed without claiming it.
2. 作而不擾，成而不誇。 Act without disturbing; complete without boasting.
3. 看而不取，聞而不爭。 See without grabbing; hear without competing.
4. 行而不逼，止而不怠。 Move without pressing; stop without collapsing.
5. 教而不壓，引而不拖。 Teach without pressing down; guide without dragging.
6. 守而不閉，用而不盡。 Guard without sealing shut; use without exhausting.
7. 取而不貪，與而不誇。 Take without greed; give without display.
8. 居而不滿，退而不失。 Dwell without self-satisfaction; withdraw without losing measure.
9. 進而不猛，緩而不廢。 Advance without charging; slow down without neglecting.
10. 知而不露，明而不耀。 Know without showing off; be clear without dazzling.
11. 蓄而不塞，發而不傷。 Store without blockage; release without harm.
12. 問而不窮，答而不滿。 Ask without cornering; answer without overfilling the claim.
13. 得而不恃，失而不亂。 Gain without depending on it; lose without falling into disorder.
14. 近而不狎，遠而不絕。 Come near without becoming careless; stand far without breaking off.
15. 視而不執，聽而不隨。 See without clinging; hear without blindly following.
16. 為而不重，止而不空。 Act without overburdening; stop without emptiness.
17. 收而不吝，放而不散。 Gather without stinginess; release without scattering.
18. 養而不縱，裁而不傷。 Nourish without indulgence; trim without damaging the root.
19. 成而不據，久而不倦。 Accomplish without occupying; continue without weariness of self-display.
20. 興而不驕，衰而不亂。 Rise without arrogance; decline without chaos.

1. 飯不香，未必不可食。 Rice that is not fragrant is not therefore inedible.
2. 門不新，未為不好門。 A door that is not new is not therefore a bad door.
3. 衣不華，未必不暖。 Clothes that are not fancy are not necessarily not warm.
4. 店不大，未必不佳。 A shop that is not large is not necessarily not good.
5. 字不多，未為無意。 Few words do not mean no meaning.
6. 路不平，未必不通。 A road that is uneven is not therefore impassable.
7. 杯不滿，未必不足。 A cup not filled to the brim is not necessarily insufficient.
8. 屋不高，未為不安。 A house that is not tall is not therefore uncomfortable.
9. 人不語，未必無心。 A person who does not speak is not necessarily without feeling.
10. 茶不濃，未必不醒。 Tea that is not strong is not necessarily not refreshing.
11. 書不厚，未必不深。 A book that is not thick is not necessarily shallow.
12. 車不快，未必不達。 A car that is not fast still may arrive.
13. 人不先，未為不成。 Not being first does not mean failure.
14. 聲不高，未必不聞。 A low voice is not necessarily unheard.
15. 屋不寬，未必不居。 A narrow house may still be livable.
16. 筆不貴，未必不善。 A pen that is not expensive is not necessarily poor.
17. 人不忙，未為無功。 Someone who does not look busy is not therefore unproductive.
18. 光不強，未必不明。 Light that is not harsh may still be clear enough.
19. 言不多，未為無見。 Few words do not mean no insight.
20. 步不疾，未必不遠。 To walk without haste does not mean one cannot go far.

1. 店有店樣，人有人情。 A shop has the form of a shop; a person has human feeling.
2. 飯有飯香，茶有茶味。 Rice has the smell of rice; tea has the taste of tea.
3. 書有書氣，字有字骨。 A book has bookish air; writing has its own backbone.
4. 門有門向，窗有窗光。 A door has its opening; a window has its light.
5. 衣有衣裁，屋有屋勢。 Clothing has its cut; a house has its structure.
6. 車有車路，人有人步。 Cars have car-roads; people have human steps.
7. 山有山色，水有水聲。 Mountains have mountain-color; water has water-sound.
8. 筆有筆意，墨有墨性。 The brush has its gesture; ink has its own nature.
9. 課有課法，學有學程。 Classes have their methods; learning has its pace.
10. 市有市聲，夜有夜靜。 The market has its noise; the night has its stillness.
11. 屋有屋影，燈有燈圈。 A house has its shadows; a lamp has its circle of light.
12. 雨有雨腳，風有風路。 Rain has its falling lines; wind too has its path.
13. 人有人氣，器有器用。 People have human presence; tools have use.
14. 茶有茶候，酒有酒性。 Tea has its proper timing; wine has its own tendency.
15. 木有木理，石有石紋。 Wood has grain; stone has veining.
16. 遠有遠意，近有近情。 Distance has one meaning; nearness has another feeling.
17. 手有手感，眼有眼力。 Hands have touch; eyes have discernment.
18. 早有早光，晚有晚色。 Morning has morning light; evening has evening color.
19. 舊有舊味，新有新意。 Old things have old flavor; new things have new intention.
20. 動有動勢，靜有靜心。 Motion has its momentum; stillness has its own mind.

1. 天冷，故添衣。 It is cold, therefore add a coat.
2. 雨近，是以收衣。 Rain is near, so the clothes are brought in.
3. 路遠，故早行。 The road is long, therefore leave early.
4. 客至，是以掃庭。 Guests are coming, so the courtyard is swept.
5. 水急，故緩步。 The water is swift, therefore walk more carefully.
6. 鍋熱，是以減火。 The pot is too hot, so the flame is lowered.
7. 夜深，故閉門。 The night is deep, therefore the door is shut.
8. 米少，是以省食。 Rice is scarce, so the meal is made modest.
9. 人倦，故少言。 People are tired, therefore speech grows sparse.
10. 事繁，是以先簡。 The tasks are many, so one simplifies first.
11. 風起，故掩窗。 Wind rises, therefore the window is closed.
12. 道滑，是以徐行。 The path is slick, so one walks slowly.
13. 燈暗，故近書。 The lamp is dim, therefore the book is brought closer.
14. 客多，是以添席。 There are many guests, so more seats are added.
15. 聲雜，故低語。 The place is noisy, therefore one speaks softly.
16. 飯熟，是以停火。 The rice is cooked, so the fire is stopped.
17. 時晚，故歸。 It is late, therefore return.
18. 紙薄，是以輕寫。 The paper is thin, so the writing must be light.
19. 人急，故多失。 When a person grows hurried, errors follow.
20. 心定，是以少亂。 When the mind is steady, disorder lessens.

5. 字不多，未為無意。 Few words do not mean no meaning.
6. 路不平，未必不通。 A road that is uneven is not therefore impassable.
7. 杯不滿，未必不足。 A cup not filled to the brim is not necessarily insufficient.
8. 屋不高，未為不安。 A house that is not tall is not therefore uncomfortable.
9. 人不語，未必無心。 A person who does not speak is not necessarily without feeling.
10. 茶不濃，未必不醒。 Tea that is not strong is not necessarily not refreshing.
11. 書不厚，未必不深。 A book that is not thick is not necessarily shallow.
12. 車不快，未必不達。 A car that is not fast still may arrive.
13. 人不先，未為不成。 Not being first does not mean failure.
14. 聲不高，未必不聞。 A low voice is not necessarily unheard.
15. 屋不寬，未必不居。 A narrow house may still be livable.
16. 筆不貴，未必不善。 A pen that is not expensive is not necessarily poor.
17. 人不忙，未為無功。 Someone who does not look busy is not therefore unproductive.
18. 光不強，未必不明。 Light that is not harsh may still be clear enough.
19. 言不多，未為無見。 Few words do not mean no insight.
20. 步不疾，未必不遠。 To walk without haste does not mean one cannot go far.

1. 良師少言，而人自明。 A good teacher speaks little, and people understand more by themselves.
2. 善醫不驚，而病自緩。 A good physician does not alarm first, and the illness settles.
3. 巧匠不躁，而器自成。 A skilled craftsperson is not restless, and the work comes together.
4. 明主不擾，而事自理。 A clear leader does not meddle, and affairs arrange themselves.
5. 善廚不爭，而味自和。 A good cook does not compete with the ingredients, and the flavors harmonize.
6. 佳友不逼，而情自近。 A true friend does not press, and closeness grows on its own.
7. 長者不誇，而眾自服。 An elder who does not boast is trusted more readily.
8. 善讀不急，而義自出。 A good reader does not rush, and the meaning emerges.
9. 良農不貪，而田自厚。 A good farmer does not grasp too hard, and the field grows rich.
10. 善商不欺，而客自來。 A good merchant does not cheat, and customers return on their own.
11. 善工不亂，而序自見。 A capable worker does not move chaotically, and order shows itself.
12. 善聽不奪，而言自盡。 A good listener does not seize the floor, and speech completes itself.
13. 善寫不飾，而意自真。 A good writer does not over-ornament, and the meaning stays true.
14. 善教不逼，而學自進。 Good teaching does not force, and learning advances by itself.
15. 善管不繁，而眾自安。 Good governance is not over-complicated, and people settle.
16. 善行不喧，而德自見。 Good action does not need noise; character shows itself.
17. 善守不閉，而室自安。 One who guards well does not merely shut things tight, and the house stays safe.
18. 善帶不拉，而眾自齊。 A good guide does not drag people along, and the group keeps together.
19. 善學不滿，而智自長。 A good learner does not feel full too soon, and understanding grows.
20. 善用不耗，而物自久。 To use things well is not to exhaust them, and so they last.

1. 天冷，故添衣。 It is cold, therefore add a coat.
2. 雨近，是以收衣。 Rain is near, so the clothes are brought in.
3. 路遠，故早行。 The road is long, therefore leave early.
4. 客至，是以掃庭。 Guests are coming, so the courtyard is swept.
5. 水急，故緩步。 The water is swift, therefore walk more carefully.
6. 鍋熱，是以減火。 The pot is too hot, so the flame is lowered.
7. 夜深，故閉門。 The night is deep, therefore the door is shut.
8. 米少，是以省食。 Rice is scarce, so the meal is made modest.
9. 人倦，故少言。 People are tired, therefore speech grows sparse.
10. 事繁，是以先簡。 The tasks are many, so one simplifies first.
11. 風起，故掩窗。 Wind rises, therefore the window is closed.
12. 道滑，是以徐行。 The path is slick, so one walks slowly.
13. 燈暗，故近書。 The lamp is dim, therefore the book is brought closer.
14. 客多，是以添席。 There are many guests, so more seats are added.
15. 聲雜，故低語。 The place is noisy, therefore one speaks softly.
16. 飯熟，是以停火。 The rice is cooked, so the fire is stopped.
17. 時晚，故歸。 It is late, therefore return.
18. 紙薄，是以輕寫。 The paper is thin, so the writing must be light.
19. 人急，故多失。 When a person grows hurried, errors follow.
20. 心定，是以少亂。 When the mind is steady, disorder lessens.

1. 店有店樣，人有人情。 A shop has the form of a shop; a person has human feeling.
2. 飯有飯香，茶有茶味。 Rice has the smell of rice; tea has the taste of tea.
3. 書有書氣，字有字骨。 A book has bookish air; writing has its own backbone.
4. 門有門向，窗有窗光。 A door has its opening; a window has its light.
5. 衣有衣裁，屋有屋勢。 Clothing has its cut; a house has its structure.
6. 車有車路，人有人步。 Cars have car-roads; people have human steps.
7. 山有山色，水有水聲。 Mountains have mountain-color; water has water-sound.
8. 筆有筆意，墨有墨性。 The brush has its gesture; ink has its own nature.
9. 課有課法，學有學程。 Classes have their methods; learning has its pace.
10. 市有市聲，夜有夜靜。 The market has its noise; the night has its stillness.
11. 屋有屋影，燈有燈圈。 A house has its shadows; a lamp has its circle of light.
12. 雨有雨腳，風有風路。 Rain has its falling lines; wind too has its path.
13. 人有人氣，器有器用。 People have human presence; tools have use.
14. 茶有茶候，酒有酒性。 Tea has its proper timing; wine has its own tendency.
15. 木有木理，石有石紋。 Wood has grain; stone has veining.
16. 遠有遠意，近有近情。 Distance has one meaning; nearness has another feeling.
17. 手有手感，眼有眼力。 Hands have touch; eyes have discernment.
18. 早有早光，晚有晚色。 Morning has morning light; evening has evening color.
19. 舊有舊味，新有新意。 Old things have old flavor; new things have new intention.
20. 動有動勢，靜有靜心。 Motion has its momentum; stillness has its own mind.

1. 茶可茶，豈好茶。 Tea that barely counts as tea — how could it already be good tea?
2. 酒可酒，安得為佳酒。 Wine that merely counts as wine — how could it be fine wine?
3. 飯可飯，何為佳飯。 A meal that merely counts as food — why call it a fine meal?
4. 屋可屋，豈安屋。 A house that barely counts as a house — how could it already be comfortable?
5. 車可車，安得為快車。 A vehicle that merely runs — how could it be a fast car?
6. 書可書，何為佳書。 A text that merely counts as a book — why call it a fine book?
7. 筆可筆，豈妙筆。 A pen that is merely a pen — how could it already be a marvelous pen?
8. 店可店，安得為好店。 A place that merely counts as a shop — how could it already be a good shop?
9. 鞋可鞋，何為佳鞋。 Shoes that merely count as shoes — why call them fine shoes?
10. 網可網，豈快網。 Internet that merely connects — how could it be fast internet?
11. 課可課，安得為好課。 A class that merely exists — how could it already be a good class?
12. 床可床，何為好床。 A bed that merely serves as a bed — why call it a good bed?
13. 屋可屋，安得為久屋。 A house that merely stands — how could it already be long-lasting?
14. 友可友，豈知己。 A friend is a friend — how could every friend be a soulmate?
15. 學可學，安得為深學。 To have studied at all — how could that alone count as deep learning?
16. 行可行，何為遠行。 To be able to walk — why call it a long journey already?
17. 聲可聲，豈妙音。 A sound is a sound — how could it already be beautiful music?
18. 字可字，安得為好字。 Writing that merely forms characters — how could it already be good writing?
19. 門可門，何為名門。 A gate is a gate — why call it a distinguished house?
20. 名可名，安得為真名。 A name that can be spoken — how could it already be the true name?

1. 生而不奪，成而不居。 Let it arise without seizing it; let it succeed without claiming it.
2. 作而不擾，成而不誇。 Act without disturbing; complete without boasting.
3. 看而不取，聞而不爭。 See without grabbing; hear without competing.
4. 行而不逼，止而不怠。 Move without pressing; stop without collapsing.
5. 教而不壓，引而不拖。 Teach without pressing down; guide without dragging.
6. 守而不閉，用而不盡。 Guard without sealing shut; use without exhausting.
7. 取而不貪，與而不誇。 Take without greed; give without display.
8. 居而不滿，退而不失。 Dwell without self-satisfaction; withdraw without losing measure.
9. 進而不猛，緩而不廢。 Advance without charging; slow down without neglecting.
10. 知而不露，明而不耀。 Know without showing off; be clear without dazzling.
11. 蓄而不塞，發而不傷。 Store without blockage; release without harm.
12. 問而不窮，答而不滿。 Ask without cornering; answer without overfilling the claim.
13. 得而不恃，失而不亂。 Gain without depending on it; lose without falling into disorder.
14. 近而不狎，遠而不絕。 Come near without becoming careless; stand far without breaking off.
15. 視而不執，聽而不隨。 See without clinging; hear without blindly following.
16. 為而不重，止而不空。 Act without overburdening; stop without emptiness.
17. 收而不吝，放而不散。 Gather without stinginess; release without scattering.
18. 養而不縱，裁而不傷。 Nourish without indulgence; trim without damaging the root.
19. 成而不據，久而不倦。 Accomplish without occupying; continue without weariness of self-display.
20. 興而不驕，衰而不亂。 Rise without arrogance; decline without chaos.

2. 作而不擾，成而不誇。 Act without disturbing; complete without boasting.
3. 看而不取，聞而不爭。 See without grabbing; hear without competing.
4. 行而不逼，止而不怠。 Move without pressing; stop without collapsing.
5. 教而不壓，引而不拖。 Teach without pressing down; guide without dragging.
6. 守而不閉，用而不盡。 Guard without sealing shut; use without exhausting.
7. 取而不貪，與而不誇。 Take without greed; give without display.
8. 居而不滿，退而不失。 Dwell without self-satisfaction; withdraw without losing measure.
9. 進而不猛，緩而不廢。 Advance without charging; slow down without neglecting.
10. 知而不露，明而不耀。 Know without showing off; be clear without dazzling.
11. 蓄而不塞，發而不傷。 Store without blockage; release without harm.
12. 問而不窮，答而不滿。 Ask without cornering; answer without overfilling the claim.
13. 得而不恃，失而不亂。 Gain without depending on it; lose without falling into disorder.
14. 近而不狎，遠而不絕。 Come near without becoming careless; stand far without breaking off.
15. 視而不執，聽而不隨。 See without clinging; hear without blindly following.
16. 為而不重，止而不空。 Act without overburdening; stop without emptiness.
17. 收而不吝，放而不散。 Gather without stinginess; release without scattering.
18. 養而不縱，裁而不傷。 Nourish without indulgence; trim without damaging the root.
19. 成而不據，久而不倦。 Accomplish without occupying; continue without weariness of self-display.
20. 興而不驕，衰而不亂。 Rise without arrogance; decline without chaos.

1. 茶可茶，豈好茶。 Tea that barely counts as tea — how could it already be good tea?
2. 酒可酒，安得為佳酒。 Wine that merely counts as wine — how could it be fine wine?
3. 飯可飯，何為佳飯。 A meal that merely counts as food — why call it a fine meal?
4. 屋可屋，豈安屋。 A house that barely counts as a house — how could it already be comfortable?
5. 車可車，安得為快車。 A vehicle that merely runs — how could it be a fast car?
6. 書可書，何為佳書。 A text that merely counts as a book — why call it a fine book?
7. 筆可筆，豈妙筆。 A pen that is merely a pen — how could it already be a marvelous pen?
8. 店可店，安得為好店。 A place that merely counts as a shop — how could it already be a good shop?
9. 鞋可鞋，何為佳鞋。 Shoes that merely count as shoes — why call them fine shoes?
10. 網可網，豈快網。 Internet that merely connects — how could it be fast internet?
11. 課可課，安得為好課。 A class that merely exists — how could it already be a good class?
12. 床可床，何為好床。 A bed that merely serves as a bed — why call it a good bed?
13. 屋可屋，安得為久屋。 A house that merely stands — how could it already be long-lasting?
14. 友可友，豈知己。 A friend is a friend — how could every friend be a soulmate?
15. 學可學，安得為深學。 To have studied at all — how could that alone count as deep learning?
16. 行可行，何為遠行。 To be able to walk — why call it a long journey already?
17. 聲可聲，豈妙音。 A sound is a sound — how could it already be beautiful music?
18. 字可字，安得為好字。 Writing that merely forms characters — how could it already be good writing?
19. 門可門，何為名門。 A gate is a gate — why call it a distinguished house?
20. 名可名，安得為真名。 A name that can be spoken — how could it already be the true name?

1. 店有店樣，人有人情。 A shop has the form of a shop; a person has human feeling.
2. 飯有飯香，茶有茶味。 Rice has the smell of rice; tea has the taste of tea.
3. 書有書氣，字有字骨。 A book has bookish air; writing has its own backbone.
4. 門有門向，窗有窗光。 A door has its opening; a window has its light.
5. 衣有衣裁，屋有屋勢。 Clothing has its cut; a house has its structure.
6. 車有車路，人有人步。 Cars have car-roads; people have human steps.
7. 山有山色，水有水聲。 Mountains have mountain-color; water has water-sound.
8. 筆有筆意，墨有墨性。 The brush has its gesture; ink has its own nature.
9. 課有課法，學有學程。 Classes have their methods; learning has its pace.
10. 市有市聲，夜有夜靜。 The market has its noise; the night has its stillness.
11. 屋有屋影，燈有燈圈。 A house has its shadows; a lamp has its circle of light.
12. 雨有雨腳，風有風路。 Rain has its falling lines; wind too has its path.
13. 人有人氣，器有器用。 People have human presence; tools have use.
14. 茶有茶候，酒有酒性。 Tea has its proper timing; wine has its own tendency.
15. 木有木理，石有石紋。 Wood has grain; stone has veining.
16. 遠有遠意，近有近情。 Distance has one meaning; nearness has another feeling.
17. 手有手感，眼有眼力。 Hands have touch; eyes have discernment.
18. 早有早光，晚有晚色。 Morning has morning light; evening has evening color.
19. 舊有舊味，新有新意。 Old things have old flavor; new things have new intention.
20. 動有動勢，靜有靜心。 Motion has its momentum; stillness has its own mind.

1. 飯不香，未必不可食。 Rice that is not fragrant is not therefore inedible.
2. 門不新，未為不好門。 A door that is not new is not therefore a bad door.
3. 衣不華，未必不暖。 Clothes that are not fancy are not necessarily not warm.
4. 店不大，未必不佳。 A shop that is not large is not necessarily not good.
5. 字不多，未為無意。 Few words do not mean no meaning.
6. 路不平，未必不通。 A road that is uneven is not therefore impassable.
7. 杯不滿，未必不足。 A cup not filled to the brim is not necessarily insufficient.
8. 屋不高，未為不安。 A house that is not tall is not therefore uncomfortable.
9. 人不語，未必無心。 A person who does not speak is not necessarily without feeling.
10. 茶不濃，未必不醒。 Tea that is not strong is not necessarily not refreshing.
11. 書不厚，未必不深。 A book that is not thick is not necessarily shallow.
12. 車不快，未必不達。 A car that is not fast still may arrive.
13. 人不先，未為不成。 Not being first does not mean failure.
14. 聲不高，未必不聞。 A low voice is not necessarily unheard.
15. 屋不寬，未必不居。 A narrow house may still be livable.
16. 筆不貴，未必不善。 A pen that is not expensive is not necessarily poor.
17. 人不忙，未為無功。 Someone who does not look busy is not therefore unproductive.
18. 光不強，未必不明。 Light that is not harsh may still be clear enough.
19. 言不多，未為無見。 Few words do not mean no insight.
20. 步不疾，未必不遠。 To walk without haste does not mean one cannot go far.

1. 天冷，故添衣。 It is cold, therefore add a coat.
2. 雨近，是以收衣。 Rain is near, so the clothes are brought in.
3. 路遠，故早行。 The road is long, therefore leave early.
4. 客至，是以掃庭。 Guests are coming, so the courtyard is swept.
5. 水急，故緩步。 The water is swift, therefore walk more carefully.
6. 鍋熱，是以減火。 The pot is too hot, so the flame is lowered.
7. 夜深，故閉門。 The night is deep, therefore the door is shut.
8. 米少，是以省食。 Rice is scarce, so the meal is made modest.
9. 人倦，故少言。 People are tired, therefore speech grows sparse.
10. 事繁，是以先簡。 The tasks are many, so one simplifies first.
11. 風起，故掩窗。 Wind rises, therefore the window is closed.
12. 道滑，是以徐行。 The path is slick, so one walks slowly.
13. 燈暗，故近書。 The lamp is dim, therefore the book is brought closer.
14. 客多，是以添席。 There are many guests, so more seats are added.
15. 聲雜，故低語。 The place is noisy, therefore one speaks softly.
16. 飯熟，是以停火。 The rice is cooked, so the fire is stopped.
17. 時晚，故歸。 It is late, therefore return.
18. 紙薄，是以輕寫。 The paper is thin, so the writing must be light.
19. 人急，故多失。 When a person grows hurried, errors follow.
20. 心定，是以少亂。 When the mind is steady, disorder lessens.

13. 燈暗，故近書。 The lamp is dim, therefore the book is brought closer.
14. 客多，是以添席。 There are many guests, so more seats are added.
15. 聲雜，故低語。 The place is noisy, therefore one speaks softly.
16. 飯熟，是以停火。 The rice is cooked, so the fire is stopped.
17. 時晚，故歸。 It is late, therefore return.
18. 紙薄，是以輕寫。 The paper is thin, so the writing must be light.
19. 人急，故多失。 When a person grows hurried, errors follow.
20. 心定，是以少亂。 When the mind is steady, disorder lessens.

1. 店有店樣，人有人情。 A shop has the form of a shop; a person has human feeling.
2. 飯有飯香，茶有茶味。 Rice has the smell of rice; tea has the taste of tea.
3. 書有書氣，字有字骨。 A book has bookish air; writing has its own backbone.
4. 門有門向，窗有窗光。 A door has its opening; a window has its light.
5. 衣有衣裁，屋有屋勢。 Clothing has its cut; a house has its structure.
6. 車有車路，人有人步。 Cars have car-roads; people have human steps.
7. 山有山色，水有水聲。 Mountains have mountain-color; water has water-sound.
8. 筆有筆意，墨有墨性。 The brush has its gesture; ink has its own nature.
9. 課有課法，學有學程。 Classes have their methods; learning has its pace.
10. 市有市聲，夜有夜靜。 The market has its noise; the night has its stillness.
11. 屋有屋影，燈有燈圈。 A house has its shadows; a lamp has its circle of light.
12. 雨有雨腳，風有風路。 Rain has its falling lines; wind too has its path.
13. 人有人氣，器有器用。 People have human presence; tools have use.
14. 茶有茶候，酒有酒性。 Tea has its proper timing; wine has its own tendency.
15. 木有木理，石有石紋。 Wood has grain; stone has veining.
16. 遠有遠意，近有近情。 Distance has one meaning; nearness has another feeling.
17. 手有手感，眼有眼力。 Hands have touch; eyes have discernment.
18. 早有早光，晚有晚色。 Morning has morning light; evening has evening color.
19. 舊有舊味，新有新意。 Old things have old flavor; new things have new intention.
20. 動有動勢，靜有靜心。 Motion has its momentum; stillness has its own mind.

1. 飯不香，未必不可食。 Rice that is not fragrant is not therefore inedible.
2. 門不新，未為不好門。 A door that is not new is not therefore a bad door.
3. 衣不華，未必不暖。 Clothes that are not fancy are not necessarily not warm.
4. 店不大，未必不佳。 A shop that is not large is not necessarily not good.
5. 字不多，未為無意。 Few words do not mean no meaning.
6. 路不平，未必不通。 A road that is uneven is not therefore impassable.
7. 杯不滿，未必不足。 A cup not filled to the brim is not necessarily insufficient.
8. 屋不高，未為不安。 A house that is not tall is not therefore uncomfortable.
9. 人不語，未必無心。 A person who does not speak is not necessarily without feeling.
10. 茶不濃，未必不醒。 Tea that is not strong is not necessarily not refreshing.
11. 書不厚，未必不深。 A book that is not thick is not necessarily shallow.
12. 車不快，未必不達。 A car that is not fast still may arrive.
13. 人不先，未為不成。 Not being first does not mean failure.
14. 聲不高，未必不聞。 A low voice is not necessarily unheard.
15. 屋不寬，未必不居。 A narrow house may still be livable.
16. 筆不貴，未必不善。 A pen that is not expensive is not necessarily poor.
17. 人不忙，未為無功。 Someone who does not look busy is not therefore unproductive.
18. 光不強，未必不明。 Light that is not harsh may still be clear enough.
19. 言不多，未為無見。 Few words do not mean no insight.
20. 步不疾，未必不遠。 To walk without haste does not mean one cannot go far.

1. 生而不奪，成而不居。 Let it arise without seizing it; let it succeed without claiming it.
2. 作而不擾，成而不誇。 Act without disturbing; complete without boasting.
3. 看而不取，聞而不爭。 See without grabbing; hear without competing.
4. 行而不逼，止而不怠。 Move without pressing; stop without collapsing.
5. 教而不壓，引而不拖。 Teach without pressing down; guide without dragging.
6. 守而不閉，用而不盡。 Guard without sealing shut; use without exhausting.
7. 取而不貪，與而不誇。 Take without greed; give without display.
8. 居而不滿，退而不失。 Dwell without self-satisfaction; withdraw without losing measure.
9. 進而不猛，緩而不廢。 Advance without charging; slow down without neglecting.
10. 知而不露，明而不耀。 Know without showing off; be clear without dazzling.
11. 蓄而不塞，發而不傷。 Store without blockage; release without harm.
12. 問而不窮，答而不滿。 Ask without cornering; answer without overfilling the claim.
13. 得而不恃，失而不亂。 Gain without depending on it; lose without falling into disorder.
14. 近而不狎，遠而不絕。 Come near without becoming careless; stand far without breaking off.
15. 視而不執，聽而不隨。 See without clinging; hear without blindly following.
16. 為而不重，止而不空。 Act without overburdening; stop without emptiness.
17. 收而不吝，放而不散。 Gather without stinginess; release without scattering.
18. 養而不縱，裁而不傷。 Nourish without indulgence; trim without damaging the root.
19. 成而不據，久而不倦。 Accomplish without occupying; continue without weariness of self-display.
20. 興而不驕，衰而不亂。 Rise without arrogance; decline without chaos.

1. 茶可茶，豈好茶。 Tea that barely counts as tea — how could it already be good tea?
2. 酒可酒，安得為佳酒。 Wine that merely counts as wine — how could it be fine wine?
3. 飯可飯，何為佳飯。 A meal that merely counts as food — why call it a fine meal?
4. 屋可屋，豈安屋。 A house that barely counts as a house — how could it already be comfortable?
5. 車可車，安得為快車。 A vehicle that merely runs — how could it be a fast car?
6. 書可書，何為佳書。 A text that merely counts as a book — why call it a fine book?
7. 筆可筆，豈妙筆。 A pen that is merely a pen — how could it already be a marvelous pen?
8. 店可店，安得為好店。 A place that merely counts as a shop — how could it already be a good shop?
9. 鞋可鞋，何為佳鞋。 Shoes that merely count as shoes — why call them fine shoes?
10. 網可網，豈快網。 Internet that merely connects — how could it be fast internet?
11. 課可課，安得為好課。 A class that merely exists — how could it already be a good class?
12. 床可床，何為好床。 A bed that merely serves as a bed — why call it a good bed?
13. 屋可屋，安得為久屋。 A house that merely stands — how could it already be long-lasting?
14. 友可友，豈知己。 A friend is a friend — how could every friend be a soulmate?
15. 學可學，安得為深學。 To have studied at all — how could that alone count as deep learning?
16. 行可行，何為遠行。 To be able to walk — why call it a long journey already?
17. 聲可聲，豈妙音。 A sound is a sound — how could it already be beautiful music?
18. 字可字，安得為好字。 Writing that merely forms characters — how could it already be good writing?
19. 門可門，何為名門。 A gate is a gate — why call it a distinguished house?
20. 名可名，安得為真名。 A name that can be spoken — how could it already be the true name?

1. 天冷，故添衣。 It is cold, therefore add a coat.
2. 雨近，是以收衣。 Rain is near, so the clothes are brought in.
3. 路遠，故早行。 The road is long, therefore leave early.
4. 客至，是以掃庭。 Guests are coming, so the courtyard is swept.
5. 水急，故緩步。 The water is swift, therefore walk more carefully.
6. 鍋熱，是以減火。 The pot is too hot, so the flame is lowered.
7. 夜深，故閉門。 The night is deep, therefore the door is shut.
8. 米少，是以省食。 Rice is scarce, so the meal is made modest.
9. 人倦，故少言。 People are tired, therefore speech grows sparse.
10. 事繁，是以先簡。 The tasks are many, so one simplifies first.
11. 風起，故掩窗。 Wind rises, therefore the window is closed.
12. 道滑，是以徐行。 The path is slick, so one walks slowly.
13. 燈暗，故近書。 The lamp is dim, therefore the book is brought closer.
14. 客多，是以添席。 There are many guests, so more seats are added.
15. 聲雜，故低語。 The place is noisy, therefore one speaks softly.
16. 飯熟，是以停火。 The rice is cooked, so the fire is stopped.
17. 時晚，故歸。 It is late, therefore return.
18. 紙薄，是以輕寫。 The paper is thin, so the writing must be light.
19. 人急，故多失。 When a person grows hurried, errors follow.
20. 心定，是以少亂。 When the mind is steady, disorder lessens.

9. 課有課法，學有學程。 Classes have their methods; learning has its pace.
10. 市有市聲，夜有夜靜。 The market has its noise; the night has its stillness.
11. 屋有屋影，燈有燈圈。 A house has its shadows; a lamp has its circle of light.
12. 雨有雨腳，風有風路。 Rain has its falling lines; wind too has its path.
13. 人有人氣，器有器用。 People have human presence; tools have use.
14. 茶有茶候，酒有酒性。 Tea has its proper timing; wine has its own tendency.
15. 木有木理，石有石紋。 Wood has grain; stone has veining.
16. 遠有遠意，近有近情。 Distance has one meaning; nearness has another feeling.
17. 手有手感，眼有眼力。 Hands have touch; eyes have discernment.
18. 早有早光，晚有晚色。 Morning has morning light; evening has evening color.
19. 舊有舊味，新有新意。 Old things have old flavor; new things have new intention.
20. 動有動勢，靜有靜心。 Motion has its momentum; stillness has its own mind.

1. 天冷，故添衣。 It is cold, therefore add a coat.
2. 雨近，是以收衣。 Rain is near, so the clothes are brought in.
3. 路遠，故早行。 The road is long, therefore leave early.
4. 客至，是以掃庭。 Guests are coming, so the courtyard is swept.
5. 水急，故緩步。 The water is swift, therefore walk more carefully.
6. 鍋熱，是以減火。 The pot is too hot, so the flame is lowered.
7. 夜深，故閉門。 The night is deep, therefore the door is shut.
8. 米少，是以省食。 Rice is scarce, so the meal is made modest.
9. 人倦，故少言。 People are tired, therefore speech grows sparse.
10. 事繁，是以先簡。 The tasks are many, so one simplifies first.
11. 風起，故掩窗。 Wind rises, therefore the window is closed.
12. 道滑，是以徐行。 The path is slick, so one walks slowly.
13. 燈暗，故近書。 The lamp is dim, therefore the book is brought closer.
14. 客多，是以添席。 There are many guests, so more seats are added.
15. 聲雜，故低語。 The place is noisy, therefore one speaks softly.
16. 飯熟，是以停火。 The rice is cooked, so the fire is stopped.
17. 時晚，故歸。 It is late, therefore return.
18. 紙薄，是以輕寫。 The paper is thin, so the writing must be light.
19. 人急，故多失。 When a person grows hurried, errors follow.
20. 心定，是以少亂。 When the mind is steady, disorder lessens.

1. 甜與苦並，味乃分明。 Sweet and bitter placed together make the taste clearer.
2. 高與下對，勢乃可見。 With high and low set against each other, the shape becomes visible.
3. 長與短較，度乃可知。 When long and short are compared, measure becomes knowable.
4. 快與慢參，程乃可辨。 Set speed against slowness and the pace becomes clear.
5. 多與少列，數乃自見。 Place many beside few and quantity shows itself.
6. 新與舊並，意乃不同。 New and old side by side create different meaning.
7. 明與暗分，光乃可覺。 Light is felt more clearly when set against dark.
8. 近與遠照，路乃成形。 Near and far together give shape to the path.
9. 熱與冷參，氣乃可察。 Heat and cold together make temperature noticeable.
10. 鬆與緊較，力乃可識。 Compare loose and tight and the force becomes legible.
11. 靜與動分，勢乃可名。 Stillness and motion set apart make the pattern nameable.
12. 先與後接，序乃自成。 First and after arranged together make sequence on their own.
13. 內與外照，界乃可守。 Inner and outer define a boundary when they face each other.
14. 寬與窄並，身乃知處。 Wide and narrow together teach the body where to stand.
15. 重與輕較，手乃知分。 Weight becomes clear only when heavy and light are compared.
16. 厚與薄列，材乃可辨。 Thickness and thinness reveal the material by contrast.
17. 前與側觀，形乃可成。 Form emerges when front and side are seen together.
18. 滿與缺對，意乃更深。 Fullness and lack deepen meaning when paired.
19. 直與曲參，路乃不單。 Straight and curved together show that a path is never only one thing.
20. 軟與硬並，用乃不同。 Soft and hard set together show different kinds of use.

1. 飯不香，未必不可食。 Rice that is not fragrant is not therefore inedible.
2. 門不新，未為不好門。 A door that is not new is not therefore a bad door.
3. 衣不華，未必不暖。 Clothes that are not fancy are not necessarily not warm.
4. 店不大，未必不佳。 A shop that is not large is not necessarily not good.
5. 字不多，未為無意。 Few words do not mean no meaning.
6. 路不平，未必不通。 A road that is uneven is not therefore impassable.
7. 杯不滿，未必不足。 A cup not filled to the brim is not necessarily insufficient.
8. 屋不高，未為不安。 A house that is not tall is not therefore uncomfortable.
9. 人不語，未必無心。 A person who does not speak is not necessarily without feeling.
10. 茶不濃，未必不醒。 Tea that is not strong is not necessarily not refreshing.
11. 書不厚，未必不深。 A book that is not thick is not necessarily shallow.
12. 車不快，未必不達。 A car that is not fast still may arrive.
13. 人不先，未為不成。 Not being first does not mean failure.
14. 聲不高，未必不聞。 A low voice is not necessarily unheard.
15. 屋不寬，未必不居。 A narrow house may still be livable.
16. 筆不貴，未必不善。 A pen that is not expensive is not necessarily poor.
17. 人不忙，未為無功。 Someone who does not look busy is not therefore unproductive.
18. 光不強，未必不明。 Light that is not harsh may still be clear enough.
19. 言不多，未為無見。 Few words do not mean no insight.
20. 步不疾，未必不遠。 To walk without haste does not mean one cannot go far.

1. 生而不奪，成而不居。 Let it arise without seizing it; let it succeed without claiming it.
2. 作而不擾，成而不誇。 Act without disturbing; complete without boasting.
3. 看而不取，聞而不爭。 See without grabbing; hear without competing.
4. 行而不逼，止而不怠。 Move without pressing; stop without collapsing.
5. 教而不壓，引而不拖。 Teach without pressing down; guide without dragging.
6. 守而不閉，用而不盡。 Guard without sealing shut; use without exhausting.
7. 取而不貪，與而不誇。 Take without greed; give without display.
8. 居而不滿，退而不失。 Dwell without self-satisfaction; withdraw without losing measure.
9. 進而不猛，緩而不廢。 Advance without charging; slow down without neglecting.
10. 知而不露，明而不耀。 Know without showing off; be clear without dazzling.
11. 蓄而不塞，發而不傷。 Store without blockage; release without harm.
12. 問而不窮，答而不滿。 Ask without cornering; answer without overfilling the claim.
13. 得而不恃，失而不亂。 Gain without depending on it; lose without falling into disorder.
14. 近而不狎，遠而不絕。 Come near without becoming careless; stand far without breaking off.
15. 視而不執，聽而不隨。 See without clinging; hear without blindly following.
16. 為而不重，止而不空。 Act without overburdening; stop without emptiness.
17. 收而不吝，放而不散。 Gather without stinginess; release without scattering.
18. 養而不縱，裁而不傷。 Nourish without indulgence; trim without damaging the root.
19. 成而不據，久而不倦。 Accomplish without occupying; continue without weariness of self-display.
20. 興而不驕，衰而不亂。 Rise without arrogance; decline without chaos.

1. 良師少言，而人自明。 A good teacher speaks little, and people understand more by themselves.
2. 善醫不驚，而病自緩。 A good physician does not alarm first, and the illness settles.
3. 巧匠不躁，而器自成。 A skilled craftsperson is not restless, and the work comes together.
4. 明主不擾，而事自理。 A clear leader does not meddle, and affairs arrange themselves.
5. 善廚不爭，而味自和。 A good cook does not compete with the ingredients, and the flavors harmonize.
6. 佳友不逼，而情自近。 A true friend does not press, and closeness grows on its own.
7. 長者不誇，而眾自服。 An elder who does not boast is trusted more readily.
8. 善讀不急，而義自出。 A good reader does not rush, and the meaning emerges.
9. 良農不貪，而田自厚。 A good farmer does not grasp too hard, and the field grows rich.
10. 善商不欺，而客自來。 A good merchant does not cheat, and customers return on their own.
11. 善工不亂，而序自見。 A capable worker does not move chaotically, and order shows itself.
12. 善聽不奪，而言自盡。 A good listener does not seize the floor, and speech completes itself.
13. 善寫不飾，而意自真。 A good writer does not over-ornament, and the meaning stays true.
14. 善教不逼，而學自進。 Good teaching does not force, and learning advances by itself.
15. 善管不繁，而眾自安。 Good governance is not over-complicated, and people settle.
16. 善行不喧，而德自見。 Good action does not need noise; character shows itself.
17. 善守不閉，而室自安。 One who guards well does not merely shut things tight, and the house stays safe.
18. 善帶不拉，而眾自齊。 A good guide does not drag people along, and the group keeps together.
19. 善學不滿，而智自長。 A good learner does not feel full too soon, and understanding grows.
20. 善用不耗，而物自久。 To use things well is not to exhaust them, and so they last.

1. 生而不奪，成而不居。 Let it arise without seizing it; let it succeed without claiming it.
2. 作而不擾，成而不誇。 Act without disturbing; complete without boasting.
3. 看而不取，聞而不爭。 See without grabbing; hear without competing.
4. 行而不逼，止而不怠。 Move without pressing; stop without collapsing.
5. 教而不壓，引而不拖。 Teach without pressing down; guide without dragging.
6. 守而不閉，用而不盡。 Guard without sealing shut; use without exhausting.
7. 取而不貪，與而不誇。 Take without greed; give without display.
8. 居而不滿，退而不失。 Dwell without self-satisfaction; withdraw without losing measure.
9. 進而不猛，緩而不廢。 Advance without charging; slow down without neglecting.
10. 知而不露，明而不耀。 Know without showing off; be clear without dazzling.
11. 蓄而不塞，發而不傷。 Store without blockage; release without harm.
12. 問而不窮，答而不滿。 Ask without cornering; answer without overfilling the claim.
13. 得而不恃，失而不亂。 Gain without depending on it; lose without falling into disorder.
14. 近而不狎，遠而不絕。 Come near without becoming careless; stand far without breaking off.
15. 視而不執，聽而不隨。 See without clinging; hear without blindly following.
16. 為而不重，止而不空。 Act without overburdening; stop without emptiness.
17. 收而不吝，放而不散。 Gather without stinginess; release without scattering.
18. 養而不縱，裁而不傷。 Nourish without indulgence; trim without damaging the root.
19. 成而不據，久而不倦。 Accomplish without occupying; continue without weariness of self-display.
20. 興而不驕，衰而不亂。 Rise without arrogance; decline without chaos.
